answer:
Short answer: **I wouldn’t put much weight on the claim that “interesting work doesn’t really affect happiness.”** The research mostly points the other way.

What *is* true is something more nuanced:

- **Interesting/intrinsically motivating work does reliably predict higher job satisfaction and well-being.**
- But **it’s not the only thing that matters**, and it’s often **not even the biggest thing once you zoom in on actual day-to-day experience**.
- People often make worse decisions when they optimize for one salient factor (“most interesting topic”, “highest pay”, “leadership title”) instead of overall fit.

So if your question is:

> “Does research show people are *not much happier* when they pick the most interesting job possible?”

I’d say: **not really**. The better-supported claim is:

> “People are happier when their job is interesting, autonomous, meaningful, and fits their values and goals — but maximizing ‘interestingness’ alone is not the same as maximizing happiness.”

## What the research says

### 1) Intrinsically interesting work matters a lot
A big body of organizational psychology supports this.

Two important frameworks:

- **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham): jobs that have **skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback** tend to produce more internal motivation and satisfaction.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan): people do better when work supports **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**.

Meta-analyses in work design and motivation generally find that jobs with these features are associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher engagement
- lower burnout
- more persistence and better performance

So the broad research consensus is **not** “interesting work barely matters.” It does matter.

### 2) But “interesting” is only one part of a bundle
This is the key correction to the popular advice.

A job can be intellectually interesting but still make you miserable if it has:

- bad manager
- chaotic org
- low autonomy
- political environment
- constant deadline stress
- unclear role
- poor colleagues
- little growth in the direction you actually want

And the reverse can also happen:

- a topic that is less intrinsically exciting can still be very satisfying if you have
  - real ownership
  - strong team
  - visible impact
  - good leadership
  - learning
  - a role aligned with your long-term identity

So the evidence suggests that **“interestingness” has a real effect, but overall fit matters more than any single attribute**.

### 3) Money helps, but usually less than people think at your level
For people already earning comfortably high incomes, extra pay still matters, but the marginal effect on well-being is usually **smaller than differences in daily work quality**.

That doesn’t mean salary is irrelevant. It means:

- if the salary difference is life-changing, it matters a lot
- if it’s “a bit higher,” it often should not dominate the decision

In your case, the salary sounds like a tie-breaker, not the main factor.

### 4) Personal goals matter: wanting to lead is not fake or secondary
This is important in your situation.

A lot of satisfaction comes from making progress toward goals that are central to your identity. If **leading a small team is something you’ve genuinely wanted for a long time**, that is not some superficial perk. It can be a major source of meaning and satisfaction.

Research on motivation and well-being would predict that people are happier when they can express valued parts of themselves. If part of your self-concept is:

- mentoring others
- setting direction
- building a team
- multiplying impact through others

then a leadership role may satisfy something deeper than topic-interest alone.

So don’t frame it as:

- “interesting work” = real happiness
- “leading” = ego/status temptation

That would be too simplistic.

### 5) People are bad at predicting what will make them happy
This part of the literature is also relevant.

Research on **affective forecasting** shows that people often overweight salient features of a choice and underweight what daily life will actually feel like.

Common errors:

- overweighting title/prestige
- overweighting salary
- overweighting topic glamour
- underweighting calendar reality
- underweighting manager/team quality
- underweighting how much the work itself changes once you move into management

This matters because “leading a small team” can mean very different things:

- coaching and technical direction
- or hiring, coordination, performance management, conflict resolution, status meetings

Likewise, “very interesting research” can mean:

- stimulating intellectual problems
- or a narrow, lonely optimization loop with little leverage

So the question is less “which option sounds better?” and more “which Monday-through-Friday experience is likely to feel better six months in?”

---

## What I think the research implies for your decision

I’d phrase your real tradeoff like this:

### Stay at Google
Pros:
- more intrinsically interesting work
- probably stronger day-to-day intellectual enjoyment
- you already know the environment
- lower execution risk

Cons:
- may delay a move into leadership
- possible future regret if you keep wanting to lead and never test it

### Go to Anthropic
Pros:
- chance to lead a team, which may be deeply important to you
- new growth trajectory
- likely more ownership / influence
- slightly higher pay

Cons:
- less interesting content
- management may reduce hands-on technical enjoyment more than you expect
- higher uncertainty

The research-based takeaway is:

> **Don’t ask “Which job is more interesting?”**  
> Ask: **“Which job better satisfies the combination of autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, and progress toward the kind of person I want to become?”**

That is much closer to what predicts satisfaction.

---

## My honest synthesis

If I had to reduce it to one sentence:

- **If leading people is a deep aspiration, Anthropic could absolutely make you happier overall even if the content is less interesting.**
- **If leading is more of a romanticized idea and what really energizes you is the work itself, you’ll probably be happier staying closer to the more interesting problems.**

The main risk is not “choosing less interesting work.”
The main risk is **misunderstanding what you actually want more: the craft or the role.**

---

## A practical test

These questions often clarify things fast:

1. **If Anthropic were the same pay as Google, would you still want it?**
   - If yes, leadership is probably the real pull.

2. **If Google offered you a small-team lead role on equally interesting work, would you stay instantly?**
   - If yes, the key issue is not company; it’s the chance to lead.

3. **If Anthropic removed the team-leading piece, would you still leave?**
   - If no, then the move is really about leadership, not company or pay.

4. **Which regret feels worse in 2 years?**
   - “I delayed leadership again”
   - or “I left fascinating work for a role I didn’t actually enjoy”

---

## One thing I’d strongly suggest
Before deciding, get concrete answers about the Anthropic role:

- How much of the week is actually management?
- How much hands-on technical work remains?
- What does success in 6–12 months look like?
- What are the people problems likely to be?
- Who would you manage?
- How much autonomy do you really have?
- What does the calendar of someone in that role actually look like?

Because research says **specific work design beats abstract job labels**.

---

If you want, I can help you make a **decision matrix grounded in the research** rather than vibes — e.g. weighting interest, leadership growth, autonomy, team quality, compensation, reversibility, and likely regret.

answer:
Short version: the research does **not** really support “interesting work doesn’t matter much.” If anything, it usually finds the opposite: **interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is consistently associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being**.

But it also does **not** support the stronger claim that you should always choose the **most interesting possible job**. Happiness at work seems to come from a **bundle**:

- interesting / meaningful work
- autonomy
- competence / growth
- good colleagues and manager
- manageable stress
- values fit
- pay that feels fair
- life outside work

So the right conclusion is usually:

> **Interestingness matters a lot, but it is only one dimension, and maximizing only that dimension is often not optimal.**

## What the research generally says

A few robust findings from organizational psychology:

### 1. Intrinsic job characteristics matter a lot
Classic work on the **Job Characteristics Model** and later research find that jobs feel better when they have things like:

- skill variety
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

These tend to increase **experienced meaningfulness** and **job satisfaction**.

So if by “interesting” you mean “engaging, meaningful, challenging,” research generally says that does matter.

### 2. Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
A huge literature finds that people do better when work supports:

- **autonomy**: I have meaningful control
- **competence**: I’m growing and effective
- **relatedness**: I feel connected / valued

Interesting work often helps with competence and intrinsic motivation, but leadership can also strongly support autonomy/competence/relatedness.

So a role can be “less interesting technically” but better overall if it gives you more ownership and a role you’ve long wanted.

### 3. Meaning / purpose predicts satisfaction, but not infinitely
People who find work meaningful generally report higher satisfaction and engagement. But there are limits:

- meaningful work can coexist with burnout
- people can tolerate bad conditions because the work feels important
- “most interesting topic” can still be miserable if the environment is bad

So meaning helps, but it is not magic.

### 4. Pay matters, but mostly up to “fair enough”
Research usually finds that pay matters a lot for:

- reducing stress
- signaling status / fairness
- giving optionality

But once pay is already high enough that you feel secure and fairly treated, **differences in day-to-day happiness are often driven more by the nature of the work and environment than by extra compensation**.

Since you’re at Google and Anthropic is only “a bit higher salary,” I would personally treat salary as a **secondary** factor unless it changes your life materially.

### 5. Person-job fit matters more than abstract job quality
Two people can react very differently to the same role.

If you have a strong latent desire to **lead a team**, then a leadership opportunity may raise your well-being more than the content-level interestingness score would suggest.

In other words:

- “Interesting research topic” is one type of fit
- “This role matches who I want to become” is another

The second one is often underrated.

---

## So is there research saying “people are not much happier when they pick the most interesting job possible”?

I’d phrase it this way:

- I’m **not aware of a strong body of evidence** saying that maximizing job interestingness has little effect on happiness.
- I **am** aware of a lot of evidence that **intrinsic motivation / meaning / autonomy matter substantially**.
- But the evidence also suggests that **maximizing interestingness alone is not the right objective**.

That is, the research is more consistent with:

> “Interesting work matters, but so do autonomy, relationships, stress, growth, and fit.”

than with:

> “Interesting work is overrated and doesn’t really affect happiness.”

---

## A useful distinction: “interesting content” vs “interesting role”

This seems especially relevant to your case.

At Google:
- more interesting work content
- maybe less leadership opportunity

At Anthropic:
- less interesting content
- more interesting role structure: leading a team

Those are different kinds of “interesting.”

A lot of people accidentally optimize for:
- “What topic is coolest?”

when they should be optimizing for:
- “What life am I trying to build?”
- “What identity am I trying to grow into?”
- “What sort of day-to-day work do I want to do?”

If you’ve “always wanted” to lead, then Anthropic may actually be more aligned with your deeper motivation, even if the immediate technical problems are less exciting.

---

## What tends to regret-proof decisions

From what we know about career satisfaction, people often regret:

- staying in a technically fascinating role that stopped them from growing in the direction they wanted
- taking a “career progression” role that looked prestigious but made their daily work dull and draining
- underweighting manager/team/culture quality

So I’d evaluate these dimensions very concretely:

### 1. How much less interesting is Anthropic, really?
Is it:
- “a little less fun”
or
- “I will be bored 5 days a week”

That difference matters enormously.

### 2. How real is the leadership opportunity?
Is it:
- actual hiring, coaching, roadmap ownership, performance management
or
- “you’ll be the senior IC who informally coordinates”

A fake leadership role is a bad trade.

### 3. Could Google give you leadership soon?
If this is the main thing you want, can you get it where you are within 6–12 months?

If yes, the tradeoff changes a lot.

### 4. What is the manager / culture quality at Anthropic?
This often matters more than people expect. A good manager + solid team can dominate topic-level interestingness in predicting happiness.

### 5. Which regret feels worse?
- “I stayed and missed my first real leadership shot.”
- “I left fascinating work for a management title and got bored.”

That emotional asymmetry is often revealing.

---

## My synthesis for your specific situation

Given what you wrote, I would **not** make this decision based on the idea that “interesting work is probably overrated according to research.” That’s not really what the literature says.

Instead, I’d think:

- **If the Anthropic work is only moderately less interesting**, and the team leadership opportunity is real, that could very plausibly increase your overall satisfaction.
- **If the work is substantially less interesting**, and “leading a small team” is more title than actual role, then staying at Google may be better.
- Since salary is only “a bit higher,” it probably should not drive the decision much unless it changes your life in a meaningful way.

My guess, based on your wording, is that your real conflict is not:
- money vs interesting work

but:
- **technical fascination vs becoming the kind of person you want to become**

And research would say both matter.

---

## A practical way to decide

Rate each option from 1–10 on:

- daily task interest
- autonomy
- growth toward long-term goals
- leadership development
- manager quality
- team quality
- stress / sustainability
- mission / values fit
- compensation fairness

Then ask two separate questions:

1. **Which job would make my next 2 years better?**
2. **Which job makes me more likely to like my life 5 years from now?**

Those answers are often different, and the tension is informative.

---

## My bottom line

No, the evidence does **not** really say that choosing the most interesting job possible has little effect on happiness.

A better evidence-based summary is:

> **Interesting work is an important contributor to job satisfaction, but not the only one, and not always the one you should maximize.**

In your case, the key question is probably whether **leading a team is itself a form of meaningful work you value enough** to compensate for somewhat less interesting technical content.

If you want, I can help you think through this as a concrete decision between the Google role and the Anthropic role using a decision matrix.

Some studies suggest that while leaders often feel more purpose and control, they also experience higher stress levels. I should tread carefully here. Job interestingness relates to intrinsic motivation, and while it’s an important aspect of work design, it doesn't guarantee happiness in isolation. Research shows that interesting work is beneficial, but its effects are influenced by factors like fit, autonomy, relationships, and stress levels.
**Examining job satisfaction factors**

I know there’s strong evidence that interest itself doesn't solely predict job satisfaction. Research shows that intrinsically motivating work often leads to higher engagement. However, people tend to underestimate how specific job features impact long-term well-being. 

For instance, while interesting work is positively associated with satisfaction, factors like meaningfulness and autonomy can be even more predictive. Also, compensation plays a role, especially when it's inadequate. Beyond that, fit and individual growth aspirations can influence satisfaction differently, particularly in management roles.
**Understanding job satisfaction findings**

Research highlights that the most objectively interesting job doesn’t always lead to maximizing happiness. People tend to adapt over time, causing novelty to fade. Additionally, obsessive passion for "interesting" jobs can harm well-being if it consumes one's identity and time.

When making decisions, I could compare experiences based on autonomy, learning, purpose, colleagues, and workload. Running a pre-mortem for two years ahead or trial leadership could be helpful. Mentioning studies indicating that time in flow and social connection are strong predictors of daily well-being might also be useful.
**Identifying job satisfaction factors**

In examining job satisfaction, I find that motivational characteristics are strongly linked to job satisfaction and engagement, as shown in meta-analyses by Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson. Other notable studies discuss the importance of meaningful work, person-job fit, and how calling impacts work.

Challenging work is often cited as a strong predictor of satisfaction. If "interesting" refers to intellectually stimulating problems, it can help, but isn’t the only factor in overall well-being. **Exploring job satisfaction factors**

**Identifying key job factors**

I think there are several important factors that really matter for job satisfaction, like autonomy, learning, meaningfulness, relationships, manageable stress, fairness, personal fit, and room for growth. The "interesting technical problem" is just one aspect. I should mention that the desire for leadership indicates that "interestingness" is multifaceted. 

Now, I need to be careful about how I suggest experiments, like shadowing a manager or negotiating for more time on projects. It might be more helpful to weigh what's more disappointing in two years: not leading or missing out on innovative research. I'll summarize my thoughts in a clear answer.
**Clarifying job satisfaction claims**

The best-supported claim I see is more focused: once pay is sufficient, people generally find greater happiness in jobs that feel intrinsically motivating and meaningful. However, this gain heavily relies on factors like autonomy, fit, relationships, and stress. 

People often overrate how much one specific feature, such as topic interest, will impact their long-term happiness. answer:
Short answer: **I don’t think the research supports “interesting work doesn’t matter much.”**  
But it also **doesn’t support “maximize interestingness at all costs.”**

The best-supported version is:

- **Interesting / intrinsically motivating work usually does increase job satisfaction**
- but **overall happiness depends on several other factors just as much or more**:
  - autonomy
  - sense of progress/mastery
  - relationships with coworkers
  - workload/stress
  - fit with your values and identity
  - fairness of compensation
  - whether the role matches what you want to become

So if you choose the “most interesting” job while sacrificing too much on those other dimensions, you may **not** end up happier.

## What the research tends to show

Researchers usually don’t study “interestingness” as a single variable. They study nearby constructs:

### 1. Intrinsic motivation / job design
Classic work-design research finds that jobs are more satisfying when they have:

- variety
- challenge
- autonomy
- feedback
- significance

That is very much in the direction of “interesting work matters.”

A well-known framework here is **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**.  
More recent meta-analyses on work design also generally find that **motivating job characteristics predict higher satisfaction and engagement**.

### 2. Meaningful work
There is also a lot of evidence that feeling your work is meaningful is associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- more engagement
- better general wellbeing

That said, **meaningful** and **interesting** are not the same thing. A role can be technically fascinating but not meaningful to you, or vice versa.

### 3. Person-job fit
A big predictor is **fit**: whether the job matches your strengths, motives, and what you want from life right now.

If one person wants deep technical exploration and another wants leadership/growth, the same job can make one thrive and the other miserable.

This matters a lot for your case, because you said **leading a small team is something you always wanted to do**. Research would treat that as a major source of fit, not as a minor side issue.

### 4. Pay matters, but with diminishing returns
Pay clearly matters for wellbeing when it affects:

- security
- fairness
- feeling valued
- life flexibility

But once compensation is already high and feels fair, the extra happiness from a somewhat higher salary is usually **smaller** than people imagine, compared with day-to-day work experience.

So the “bit higher salary” from Anthropic probably matters less than:
- whether you’ll enjoy your weeks,
- whether you’ll grow in the direction you want,
- and whether the role will energize or drain you.

## Is there evidence people overrate “interestingness”?

Yes, in a more indirect way.

There is a lot of research on **affective forecasting** showing that people are often bad at predicting what will make them happy long-term. We tend to over-focus on one salient feature of a choice and underweight:

- adaptation
- daily hassles
- team quality
- manager quality
- stress
- loss of autonomy
- whether our actual day-to-day tasks are enjoyable

So if someone says “I must maximize interest above all else,” research would give some reason to be skeptical. Not because interesting work doesn’t matter, but because **people often overweight one vivid attribute** and underweight the rest.

## A subtle but important point for your situation

You may be framing this as:

- Google = more interesting work
- Anthropic = less interesting work

But there may actually be **two different kinds of interesting** here:

1. **interest in the domain/problem**
2. **interest in the role/identity** — e.g. leading, mentoring, setting direction, building a team

If you’ve long wanted to lead, then the Anthropic role may be less interesting in content but **more interesting in trajectory**.

Research would not say content-interest is automatically more important than growth-interest.

## My synthesis

If your question is:

> “Does research show that choosing the most technically interesting job possible does not necessarily maximize happiness?”

Then the answer is **yes, that is broadly consistent with the research**.

More precise version:

- **Interesting work tends to improve job satisfaction**
- but **it is only one contributor**
- and **people often mispredict its long-run effect**
- especially when the alternative offers more:
  - autonomy
  - leadership
  - growth
  - status
  - ownership
  - alignment with long-term goals

So I would not use “interestingness” as the sole decision criterion.

## A practical way to think about your choice

Instead of asking:

> “Which job is more interesting?”

ask:

> “Which job gives me the better total package of day-to-day experience and long-term identity?”

You could score each option from 1–10 on:

- intellectual interest
- autonomy
- growth
- leadership development
- colleagues
- mission alignment
- stress / sustainability
- compensation
- prestige / future option value
- probability you’ll still like it in 2 years

Then weight the categories by what actually matters to you.

For many people at your stage, **leadership opportunity + strong option value** can outweigh some loss in technical interest. For others, giving up highly engaging work is a big mistake. The right answer depends heavily on whether your real aspiration is:

- “I want to do the most exciting research,” or
- “I want to become someone who leads teams building important things.”

Those are not the same life.

## My tentative take on your specific case

From what you wrote, the biggest risk is not that Anthropic pays a bit more or less. It’s this:

- If you stay at Google, will you regret **not trying leadership**?
- If you go to Anthropic, will you regret **giving up work you find genuinely exciting**?

Whichever regret feels more durable is probably the more important signal.

If you want, I can help you do this in a more structured way — for example:
- a decision matrix,
- a “2-year regret test,”
- or a research-based checklist of job factors that predict satisfaction.

answer:
Short answer: **the research mostly cuts against** the idea that “interesting work doesn’t matter much.”  
If anything, **the nature of the work itself** is usually one of the **strongest predictors of job satisfaction**.

But there’s an important nuance:

- **Interesting work strongly affects job satisfaction**
- **It affects overall life happiness only moderately**
- **It is not the only thing that matters**
- And **“interesting” in topic** is not the same as **“interesting” in role/growth/identity**

So the evidence is less “pick the most interesting job possible at all costs,” and more:

> “Make sure your job is interesting/meaningful enough, but don’t ignore autonomy, manager quality, growth, colleagues, workload, and fit.”

## What the research generally finds

### 1. “The work itself” matters a lot
A large body of organizational psychology finds that people are happier at work when jobs have things like:

- skill variety
- challenge
- autonomy
- task significance
- feedback
- opportunities to learn/grow

This goes back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analyses like **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**. These studies generally find that enriched, engaging work is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher motivation
- better engagement
- lower burnout/withdrawal

So if by “interesting” you mean “intellectually engaging, challenging, meaningful, uses my strengths,” the literature usually says **yes, that matters a lot**.

### 2. Job satisfaction and life satisfaction are related, but not identical
Meta-analyses typically find a **moderate positive relationship** between job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction. In plain English:

- loving your job helps your life
- but it does not determine your whole happiness

This means a less interesting job may not make you miserable overall if the rest of life is great. But it also means spending 40+ hours/week on work you find dull usually isn’t nothing.

### 3. Pay matters, but usually less than people think once you’re already well-paid
Research on income and well-being generally finds:

- pay matters a lot when it affects security and freedom
- after that, marginal increases help less
- pay tends to predict satisfaction less strongly than things like autonomy, meaning, and day-to-day work quality

So if the Anthropic offer is only “a bit higher salary,” the data would usually suggest: **don’t overweight that** unless it meaningfully changes your life.

### 4. Meaning/interest is good, but “maximizing interest” is not always optimal
This is probably the closest to what you’re asking.

The evidence does **not** really say:
> “People are no happier when they choose the most interesting possible job.”

But it **does** support something like:
> “Beyond a threshold of having work that is interesting enough, other variables become very important, and maximizing topic-interest alone may not maximize well-being.”

That is:

- A fascinating topic with a bad manager, bad hours, low autonomy, or no growth can be worse
- A somewhat less fascinating topic with more ownership, better colleagues, and a role you’ve wanted for years can be better

## A key point in your case: leadership may itself be an intrinsic good
You framed this as:

- Google = more interesting work
- Anthropic = less interesting work, but more money and chance to lead a small team

Research would say: **don’t classify leadership purely as an extrinsic perk**.

If you’ve “always wanted to lead a small team,” that may satisfy needs like:

- autonomy
- impact
- growth
- identity
- mastery in a new domain

Those are all things that are strongly tied to satisfaction.

So this is not necessarily:
- “interesting work” vs “salary”

It may actually be:
- **“interesting technical content”** vs **“interesting career shape / identity / growth”**

That distinction matters a lot.

## What I think the literature implies for your decision

### If you stay at Google mainly because the research is more interesting:
That is a **real and valid reason**. Research suggests you probably would feel that difference.

### If you go to Anthropic mainly because:
- you truly want to learn leadership
- you want broader ownership
- the team seems strong
- the manager is excellent
- the work is less exciting but still “interesting enough”

…then research would not say you are making some obvious mistake. That could easily produce **equal or greater satisfaction**, especially if leadership is a genuine aspiration rather than just status.

### If Anthropic is “much less interesting” in a way that feels like boredom:
Then the higher pay probably won’t compensate much, unless the leadership opportunity is deeply important to you.

## The most evidence-based way to think about it
Instead of asking:

> “Which job is more interesting?”

ask:

> “Which job better satisfies the drivers of durable job satisfaction?”

A good list would be:

1. **Interest in day-to-day work**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Learning/growth**
4. **People/manager quality**
5. **Meaning/impact**
6. **Workload/stress**
7. **Role fit with the person I want to become**
8. **Compensation/security**
9. **Option value / future career capital**

And one subtle but important one:

10. **Reversibility**  
   If you try leadership now, can you go back to being a more individual-contributor research person later? If yes, the risk is lower.

## My honest synthesis
If you want the empirical answer to your exact question, it’s roughly this:

- **No, research does not strongly support the claim that choosing the most interesting work barely affects happiness.**
- In fact, **interesting/meaningful/challenging work usually matters quite a bit** for job satisfaction.
- But **maximizing topic-interest alone is not what the evidence recommends either**.
- For senior, already-well-paid people, **fit, autonomy, growth, colleagues, and identity** often matter as much or more than a salary bump.

So the real question may be:

> Is the Anthropic role “less interesting but still interesting enough,” while giving you a kind of growth you genuinely want?

If yes, it may be a very good move.

If instead it feels like:
> “I would be paying a lot of my weekly life to do work I care much less about, for slightly more money and a title I’m not sure I actually want,”

then the literature would suggest being cautious.

If you want, I can help you build a **decision framework** for this specific Google vs Anthropic tradeoff, rather than just the general research view.

answer:
Short version: the research does **not** really support “interesting work is overrated.” If anything, it tends to show the opposite:

- **Intrinsic job qualities** — interesting work, autonomy, learning, sense of progress, meaning — are among the **strongest predictors of job satisfaction**.
- **Pay** matters, but once you’re already well-compensated, **incremental pay increases usually have a smaller effect** on happiness than people expect.
- The catch is that **“most interesting possible” is not the same as “best for wellbeing.”** Other things matter a lot too: autonomy, colleagues, manager, workload, stress, identity, and whether the role fits who you want to become.

So if you’re asking, “Are there results showing people aren’t much happier when they pick the most interesting job possible?” I’d say:

> **Not really in that broad form.**  
> The evidence is more like: **interesting work matters a lot, but it is only one major ingredient.**

## What the literature generally says

A few well-established themes from organizational psychology:

### 1. Intrinsic features of work are strongly tied to satisfaction
Classic work design research (Hackman & Oldham; later meta-analyses) finds that jobs with more:

- variety
- autonomy
- feedback
- significance
- opportunities to use skill

produce more **motivation and job satisfaction**.

“Interesting” isn’t always measured with that exact word, but it overlaps heavily with these constructs.

### 2. Person-job fit matters a lot
A job can be objectively prestigious or exciting but still be bad for *you* if it doesn’t fit your strengths or motivations.

Research on person-job/person-organization fit consistently finds that fit predicts:

- job satisfaction
- commitment
- lower burnout/turnover

So if “interesting” to you means “I naturally want to think about this even off-hours,” that’s not trivial — it’s a big source of fit.

### 3. Pay has diminishing returns for job happiness
There’s a fairly robust pattern that **pay correlates with job satisfaction**, but usually **not as strongly** as intrinsic job characteristics do, especially once basic financial needs are already covered.

For someone choosing between two already elite jobs, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely to dominate the decision from a happiness perspective unless it changes something meaningful like:

- financial independence timeline
- family security
- geography
- flexibility

### 4. People often mispredict what will make them happy
This is probably the closest thing to what you’re asking.

People often overweight:

- salary
- prestige
- title
- the excitement of a change

and underweight:

- daily task enjoyment
- social environment
- manager quality
- stress/load
- what the average Tuesday feels like

So the research-based warning is **not** “interesting work doesn’t matter.”  
It’s more: **don’t confuse a compelling narrative about a job with the lived experience of doing it.**

## The subtle point relevant to your case

Your decision isn’t just:

- interesting work vs slightly more money

It’s more like:

- **interesting IC/research work**
vs
- **less interesting domain/content, but leadership growth, slightly more pay, and maybe more impact**

That’s important because **leading a team may itself satisfy deep intrinsic motives**:

- growth
- autonomy
- influence
- mentoring
- building something

So Anthropic may be “less interesting” in one sense but **more aligned with another long-term desire**.

The core question is:

> Which kind of interest matters more to you right now:
> **interest in the work itself**, or **interest in the role you are becoming**?

## A useful distinction: task-interest vs identity-interest

These often get mixed together.

### Task-interest
“Do I enjoy the actual day-to-day problems I work on?”

This is hugely predictive of everyday satisfaction.

### Identity-interest
“Is this the kind of person I want to become — a leader, builder, manager, founder-type?”

This is more about longer-term meaning and career arc.

A role can be low on one and high on the other.

That’s why some people leave a fascinating technical role for management and are happier — and others regret it badly.

## If I were translating the evidence into advice

I’d say:

### If the Google work is genuinely much more interesting on a daily basis
That is a **real** source of happiness, not a fluffy ideal. Don’t discount it.

### If leading a team is a serious long-term aspiration
That is also a **real** source of fulfillment, not just status. And opportunities to lead a small team in a strong environment are valuable.

### The salary difference should probably get the least weight
Unless it materially changes your life.

## A practical way to think about it

Try scoring each job on these dimensions from 1–10:

- Daily intellectual interest
- Autonomy
- Learning/growth
- Leadership development
- Colleagues you admire
- Manager quality
- Stress / sustainability
- Mission alignment
- Career option value 2–3 years from now
- Compensation

Then weight them by importance to you.

For someone in your position, I’d especially focus on:

1. **What will my average week feel like?**
2. **What skills/identity am I trying to build over the next 3 years?**
3. **If novelty wears off after 6 months, which job leaves me more energized?**

## My guess, based on research and your description

A “bit higher salary” should not drive this.

The real tradeoff is:

- **interestingness now**
vs
- **leadership growth now**

If you have “always wanted” to lead a small team, Anthropic might be worth serious consideration **even if the work itself is less interesting** — but only if:

- you’d actually spend meaningful time leading/mentoring/setting direction,
- the team and manager are strong,
- and the role isn’t just “more meetings + less interesting work.”

Because the failure mode here is obvious:  
you give up work you love, get only a shallow version of leadership, and end up less happy.

## Bottom line

The evidence suggests:

- **Interesting work usually does matter a lot for happiness.**
- There is **not strong support** for the claim that people aren’t much happier when they choose more interesting work.
- But **maximizing interestingness alone** is not the right objective; wellbeing depends on the whole package.
- In your specific case, the leadership opportunity may be a genuine intrinsic benefit, not merely an extrinsic one.

If you want, I can help you think through this decision in a more structured way — for example by building a **research-backed decision matrix** tailored to your Google vs Anthropic choice.

I’ve found evidence in the "person-job fit" literature that shows meaningful work can become detrimental when it involves overwork or exploitation. While interesting jobs do matter, their benefits may be smaller than expected, especially when weighed against factors like autonomy, team quality, and opportunities for growth.
**Reviewing job satisfaction theories**

First, there’s the Hackman & Oldham Job Characteristics Model, emphasizing skill variety and task significance that lead to meaningfulness, which boosts motivation and satisfaction. A meta-analysis by Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson in 2007 supports that motivational characteristics relate strongly to job satisfaction. Then, there's Self-Determination Theory from Deci and Ryan, focusing on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. answer:
Short answer: **the research does not support “maximize interestingness at all costs.”** But it also does **not** support the opposite claim that interesting work barely matters.

The best-supported view is:

- **Interesting / meaningful work does raise job satisfaction**
- but **its effect is only one part of the picture**
- and in many cases **autonomy, fit, manager quality, colleagues, stress, and feeling effective/progressive matter just as much or more**

So if you’re asking, “Is there evidence that choosing the most interesting possible job is not necessarily the happiness-maximizing move?” — **yes, broadly**.  
If you’re asking, “Does research show that interestingness hardly matters?” — **no, not really**.

## What the research says

### 1. More interesting/meaningful work usually does help
Classic work design research shows that jobs with more:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **task identity**
- **autonomy**
- **feedback**

tend to produce more motivation and satisfaction.

Relevant sources:
- **Hackman & Oldham** — Job Characteristics Model
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)** — meta-analysis on work design

So the “interesting work matters” people are **not making it up**.

### 2. But “interestingness” is not the dominant variable people often imagine
A big lesson from organizational psychology is that people often talk about jobs in terms of the **headline feature** (“AI safety research,” “leading a team,” “FAANG,” etc.), but happiness depends more on the **lived structure of the job**:

- how much control you have
- whether you like your coworkers
- whether your manager is good
- whether you are learning
- whether the work feels tractable
- whether you can make progress without constant friction
- whether stress is chronic

This is where **Self-Determination Theory** is useful: well-being tends to be higher when work supports:
- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A job can be intellectually fascinating but miserable if it undermines those.

### 3. “Fit” is often more predictive than abstract job attractiveness
There’s strong evidence on **person-job fit** and **person-organization fit**: people are happier and stay longer when the role matches their values, strengths, and preferences.

Relevant source:
- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005)** — meta-analysis on person-environment fit

This matters for your case because “interesting” in the abstract may be less important than:
- Do you actually want to **lead people**?
- Do you want to spend more time on hiring, alignment, coaching, prioritization?
- Do you enjoy being responsible for others’ output?
- Does the Anthropic environment fit you culturally?

If the answer is yes, then the “less interesting topic” might still be the **better-fit** job and thus the happier one.

### 4. Day-to-day progress matters a lot
Research on knowledge workers suggests that a major driver of positive day-to-day work experience is **making progress in meaningful work**.

Relevant source:
- **Amabile & Kramer** on progress and “inner work life”

This is important because a job can sound exciting but feel bad if:
- progress is slow
- politics are high
- meetings dominate
- success is ambiguous
- your best hours are spent on coordination instead of creation

That’s highly relevant to moving from an IC research role into team leadership.

### 5. Extra money helps, but usually less than people expect once you’re already well-paid
The income/well-being literature is messy, but the broad conclusion is:

- money does improve well-being, especially when it reduces real constraints
- at already high income levels, the gains are usually **smaller** than people forecast
- differences in daily experience are often driven more by work conditions than by incremental compensation

Relevant sources:
- **Kahneman & Deaton (2010)**
- **Killingsworth (2021)**

For someone already at Google-level compensation, a “bit higher salary” is probably **not irrelevant**, but it is less likely to dominate the decision unless it meaningfully changes your life.

## So is there evidence that people are *not much happier* when they pick the most interesting job possible?

I’d phrase it this way:

> There is evidence that **people are not maximally happy simply by maximizing interestingness**, because happiness at work depends heavily on autonomy, fit, social environment, progress, and stress.

But:

> There is **not** strong evidence that interestingness is mostly fake or unimportant.

So the truth is somewhere in the middle:
- **Interesting work helps**
- **It is not sufficient**
- **It is often over-weighted in career advice**

## How this applies to your exact choice

Your situation is especially tricky because the tradeoff is not just:

- interesting work vs less interesting work

It’s also:

- **IC research** vs **first leadership opportunity**
- staying in a role you know is stimulating vs stepping into a role that may match a long-term identity you care about

That changes the analysis a lot.

If leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do,” then the Anthropic role may not actually be “less interesting” in the psychologically relevant sense. It may be:

- less interesting in **topic content**
- more interesting in **personal growth / identity / responsibility**

Research would say those are both real forms of value.

## A better decision question

Instead of asking:

> “Which job is objectively more interesting?”

ask:

> “Which job will give me a better average Tuesday?”

Meaning:
- Which one gives more energy rather than drains it?
- Which one gives more autonomy?
- Which one gives more progress?
- Which one fits the person I want to become?
- Which pain would I resent less: boredom or managerial drag?

## A practical framework

Try scoring each option from 1–10 on:

1. **Daily intrinsic interest**
2. **Autonomy/control**
3. **Learning/growth**
4. **Progress/tractability**
5. **People/manager quality**
6. **Stress / sustainability**
7. **Identity fit** (“is this who I want to become?”)
8. **Option value** (how reversible is it?)
9. **Compensation**
10. **Regret if not chosen**

For people in your position, I’d usually give **extra weight** to:
- daily intrinsic interest
- autonomy
- identity fit
- reversibility

## My honest synthesis

If you want the research-based answer, it’s:

- **No, the literature does not say “always choose the most interesting work.”**
- **Yes, there is substantial reason to think people overestimate the happiness payoff of maximizing interestingness alone.**
- But **giving up genuinely absorbing work is still costly**, and many people underestimate that too.

So this is probably not a question of “interesting work vs happiness.”  
It’s more likely a question of:

> “Which kind of interest matters more to me right now: fascination with the work itself, or fascination with becoming a leader?”

If you want, I can help you build a **decision matrix tailored to Google research IC vs Anthropic team lead**, with weights and likely failure modes for each path.

answer:
Short version: **the research does not really support the idea that “interesting work barely matters.”** If anything, it usually finds the opposite:

- work that is **intrinsically interesting**
- gives **autonomy**
- uses your skills
- feels **meaningful**
- and fits your values

is associated with **higher job satisfaction, engagement, and somewhat better overall well-being**.

But the second half is important: **“most interesting possible” is not the same as “best for happiness.”** The gains from more-interesting work are real, but they are not infinite, and they can easily be offset by other factors like manager quality, stress, hours, lack of growth, or poor fit.

## What the literature broadly says

A few robust themes from organizational psychology:

### 1. Intrinsic motivation matters a lot
Research from **Self-Determination Theory** and related work finds that people do better psychologically when work supports:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

Interesting work usually boosts the first two. This tends to increase satisfaction and reduce burnout.

### 2. “Good job design” predicts satisfaction
The classic **Job Characteristics Model** says jobs are better when they have:

- skill variety
- task identity
- task significance
- autonomy
- feedback

“Interestingness” overlaps a lot with these. Meta-analytic work generally finds these features matter quite a bit for job satisfaction and motivation.

### 3. Person-job fit matters
A major finding in the literature is that **fit** matters more than abstract prestige. If a job matches your strengths, motives, and identity, people tend to be happier and stay longer.

This is relevant for you because “leading a small team” may actually be part of your fit, not just a perk.

### 4. Job satisfaction affects life satisfaction, but not perfectly
Work is a big part of life, so job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction are positively related. But the relationship is only **moderate**, not total. So:

- a more interesting job often makes you **more satisfied with work**
- but it may only make you **somewhat happier overall**

That may be the intuition you’re picking up on.

## So is there evidence that maximizing interestingness doesn’t help much?

The nuanced answer is:

**There is evidence of diminishing returns, adaptation, and tradeoffs.**
Not strong evidence that interesting work doesn’t matter, but good evidence that **maximizing only that variable is not optimal**.

A few reasons:

### 1. Hedonic adaptation
Even very interesting work becomes normal over time. The novelty fades.

So if one role is “much more interesting” on paper, that difference may feel smaller after 12 months than it does now.

### 2. Daily experience is multidimensional
People often say “I want interesting work,” but what they actually experience day to day is:

- meetings
- coworkers
- manager quality
- stress
- autonomy
- whether they feel effective
- whether they’re growing

A slightly less interesting domain with better autonomy and growth can produce more well-being than a fascinating domain with frustrating execution.

### 3. Meaningful aspiration can outweigh topic-interest
You said leading a small team is “something I always wanted to do.”

That matters. If leadership is a genuine aspiration, then Anthropic is not just “less interesting work for more money.” It may be:

- identity-congruent
- growth-enhancing
- future-option-creating

Research would predict that making progress on an important personal goal can be very satisfying, even if the immediate tasks are less inherently interesting.

### 4. Highly interesting work can have hidden downsides
There’s also research suggesting people can tolerate worse conditions in jobs they find meaningful or exciting. Sometimes interesting work leads people to accept:

- overwork
- blurred boundaries
- undercompensation relative to effort
- identity fusion with work

So “most interesting possible” is not always best for well-being.

## Applied to your situation

I’d frame the choice less as:

- **interesting work vs more money**

and more as:

- **current intrinsic technical interest**
vs
- **leadership growth + compensation + new option value**

That’s a more realistic comparison.

### A key question:
When you imagine Anthropic, is the work merely “less interesting,” or do you expect it to be **actively draining**?

Those are very different.

- If it’s **still pretty interesting**, just not maximally so, then the leadership opportunity may easily dominate.
- If it feels **viscerally uninteresting** in a way that will make Mondays painful, that is a serious negative and shouldn’t be hand-waved away.

## A practical way to decide

Try scoring both roles from 1–10 on these:

- intellectual interest
- autonomy
- learning/growth
- leadership development
- manager/team quality
- mission alignment
- compensation
- hours/stress
- future option value
- “how much would I want a normal Tuesday here?”

That last one is important. People often overweight the abstract glamour of a role and underweight the lived texture of an average day.

## My best synthesis

If you’re asking strictly about research:

- **No, the evidence does not say interesting work barely affects happiness.**
- **Yes, it does say interesting/intrinsically motivating work usually improves job satisfaction and well-being.**
- **But no, it does not imply that you should always choose the maximally interesting job.** Once a role is “interesting enough,” other variables can matter just as much or more.

In your case, the strongest reason to consider Anthropic is not the salary bump; it’s that it lets you test a path you’ve “always wanted”: leading a small team. That could be a bigger long-term source of satisfaction than preserving maximum technical interest right now.

If you want, I can help you think through this in a more decision-theoretic way—e.g. a framework specifically for **Google interesting IC role vs Anthropic less-interesting but first leadership role**.

answer:
Short answer: **the research does not really support “interesting work barely matters.”** If anything, it usually finds the opposite: people who experience their work as interesting, meaningful, autonomy-supporting, and aligned with their strengths tend to report **higher job satisfaction and somewhat higher overall well-being**.

But there’s an important nuance:

- **Interestingness matters**
- **Maximizing interestingness alone is not usually what maximizes happiness**

That second point may be closer to what you’re intuitively getting at.

## What the research generally shows

### 1. “Interesting work” is a strong predictor of job satisfaction
In organizational psychology, “interesting” is usually captured indirectly through things like:

- **skill variety**
- **task significance**
- **autonomy**
- **opportunities to learn**
- **intrinsic motivation**
- **person–job fit**
- **meaningfulness**

Classic work like **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**, and later meta-analyses like **Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson (2007)**, show that these features are reliably associated with:

- higher job satisfaction
- higher motivation
- more engagement
- lower burnout / withdrawal

So if someone chooses a job that is genuinely more intrinsically engaging, that usually **does help**.

### 2. But the effect is larger on “job satisfaction” than on “life happiness”
This is where people often overstate things.

Research on **job satisfaction vs life satisfaction** generally finds a positive relationship, but not a perfect one. Your work matters a lot, especially if you work long hours, but your overall happiness is also heavily shaped by:

- relationships
- health
- sleep
- stress level
- commute / flexibility
- manager quality
- work-life boundaries
- sense of future growth

So the jump from “more interesting work” to “much happier life” is often **real but moderate**, not magical.

### 3. People often overweight one salient feature
There’s also a separate body of research on **affective forecasting** and **hedonic adaptation** showing that people are bad at predicting what will make them happy long-term.

We tend to over-focus on one vivid attribute:

- “This research area is fascinating”
- “This company is more prestigious”
- “This role pays more”
- “I’ll finally be a manager”

and underweight the mundane things that affect day-to-day well-being more:

- who your manager is
- how much chaos there is
- whether you feel effective
- how often you’re in conflict
- whether the pace is sustainable
- whether you’re gaining options for the future

So there *is* research supporting the idea that people may **overestimate** how much one job attribute—like “interestingness”—will determine happiness.

### 4. “Most interesting possible” is not necessarily optimal
There are at least four reasons:

#### a) Interest can fade
Novelty wears off. Work that seems thrilling from the outside can become normal.

#### b) Other needs matter just as much
Self-determination theory suggests that well-being depends a lot on:

- **autonomy**
- **competence**
- **relatedness**

A job can be intellectually interesting but still bad for well-being if it leaves you powerless, ineffective, isolated, or politically boxed in.

#### c) Growth can be intrinsically rewarding too
You say leading a small team is something you’ve always wanted. That matters. You may be comparing:

- **interesting subject matter**
vs
- **interesting mode of work / career development**

Those are different kinds of interest.

A less interesting technical domain can still be deeply satisfying if it gives you:
- leadership
- ownership
- faster growth
- broader impact
- more influence

#### d) There may be a non-linear effect
Some “interesting” jobs are also more ambiguous, demanding, and cognitively draining. Challenge is good up to a point; too much can become stress.

So “maximally interesting” is not always “maximally happy.”

---

## So is there research showing people are **not much happier** when they pick the most interesting job?

I’d phrase it this way:

- **No strong research says interesting work doesn’t matter much.**
- **Yes, there is good reason to think people overestimate how much maximizing interestingness alone will improve happiness.**

That’s probably the most accurate answer.

If anything, the research suggests:

> The best outcomes come not from maximizing one dimension, but from getting a strong overall fit across interest, autonomy, growth, social environment, and sustainability.

---

## For your specific choice

Your decision is not really:

- “interesting work” vs “boring work”

It sounds more like:

- **Google:** more intellectually interesting content, less leadership
- **Anthropic:** less interesting content, but leadership, more money, maybe faster growth

That’s a much harder and more interesting tradeoff.

A useful way to think about it is to separate **content interest** from **role interest**.

Ask yourself:

### 1. What do you actually enjoy more on a weekly basis?
Not in theory—in lived experience.

Would you rather spend your week:
- doing fascinating technical work yourself
or
- coaching people, setting direction, unblocking others, hiring, aligning?

A lot of people romanticize management, then discover they miss building. Others discover that leadership is much more satisfying than technical depth.

### 2. Which regret feels bigger in 2 years?
- “I stayed and missed my chance to lead.”
- “I left the work I loved for a role that looked good on paper.”

Regret-minimization is often more useful here than trying to calculate happiness precisely.

### 3. Which option increases future optionality?
A first team lead role at Anthropic could be valuable career capital even if the domain is less interesting.

### 4. Can you get the missing piece at Google?

## My bottom line

- but **not enough that it should automatically dominate every other factor**
- and people often **overweight it relative to autonomy, manager quality, growth, and fit**

answer:
Short answer: **yes, the research is broadly consistent with your intuition**.

There is good evidence that **interesting/intrinsically motivating work increases job satisfaction**, but **not nearly enough to justify “maximize interestingness at all costs”**. Once a job is already reasonably stimulating, other factors often matter as much or more for happiness.

### What the research tends to show

A few robust themes:

- **Interesting work helps.**
  Classic work on job design and more recent motivation research both find that people are happier and more engaged when work is:
  - varied
  - challenging
  - meaningful
  - allows skill use
  - gives autonomy and feedback

- But **“interestingness” is only one component**.
  The strongest recurring drivers of job satisfaction/well-being are usually things like:
  - **autonomy/control**
  - **competence/growth**
  - **good colleagues / good manager**
  - **manageable workload**
  - **fair pay**
  - **value fit / meaning**
  - **job security**
  
  So if Job A is more intellectually exciting but Job B gives you more autonomy, growth, leadership, and money, it is *not at all obvious* that A will make you happier.

- **People adapt.**
  The “wow, this work is so interesting” effect often fades somewhat with time.  
  By contrast, chronic annoyances like bad culture, low control, unclear expectations, or constant stress often keep hurting.

- **Salary matters, but not infinitely.**
  Higher pay usually helps more with **life satisfaction** than with day-to-day emotional experience, especially once basic financial concerns are covered. So “a bit higher salary” is a real positive, but probably not the main issue here unless it materially changes your life.

- **Management/leadership is mixed.**
  Leading a team can increase:
  - meaning
  - influence
  - growth
  - status
  - autonomy

  But it can also reduce:
  - time spent on the technical work you love
  - flow states
  - clarity
  - freedom from people problems

  People who genuinely enjoy coaching, prioritization, and responsibility often like it. People who mostly want “leadership” as a badge often don’t.

### So is there evidence that “choosing the most interesting possible job” does **not** make people much happier?

I’d phrase it like this:

> Research does **not** support the idea that happiness is maximized by optimizing for interestingness alone.

It supports a softer claim:

> Interesting work is beneficial, but beyond a threshold, the marginal gain from “even more interesting” may be smaller than the gain from better fit, autonomy, growth, relationships, or life structure.

That seems especially relevant in your case, because this is **not**:
- fascinating job vs soul-crushing job

It sounds more like:
- **very interesting IC role**
vs
- **somewhat less interesting role with leadership opportunity + somewhat more pay**

That is exactly the kind of tradeoff where “interestingness is everything” is usually too simplistic.

### A subtle point in your situation

You may be framing this as:

- **interesting work** vs **less interesting work**

But the real tradeoff may be:

- **current intellectual stimulation**
vs
- **a role that fits your longer-term identity**

If you’ve “always wanted to lead a small team,” then the Anthropic role may actually be **more aligned with what you want to become**, even if the immediate technical content is less exciting.

That matters. People are often happiest not when the current task is maximally interesting, but when their work feels like it is moving them toward a self they endorse.

### A practical way to think about it

Try scoring both roles from 1–10 on these dimensions:

- Interesting daily work
- Autonomy
- Learning/growth
- Leadership development
- Colleagues / manager quality
- Mission fit
- Workload sustainability
- Compensation
- Future option value
- Regret if you decline

Two especially important questions:

1. **If you stay at Google for 2 more years, will you regret not trying leadership?**
2. **If you go to Anthropic for 2 years, will you regret giving up more interesting technical work?**

And one more:

3. **How reversible is each path?**

If taking the Anthropic role gives you valuable leadership experience and you can later return to more interesting technical work, that’s very different from permanently moving away from what you love.

### My bottom line

I would not trust the simplistic advice that “you must choose the most interesting work available.”

A better reading of the evidence is:

- **Interesting work matters**
- but **it is only one contributor to happiness**
- and in high-end careers, once both options are already good, **fit and trajectory often matter more than marginal interestingness**

So your question should probably be less:

- “Will I be happier doing the most interesting thing?”

and more:

- “Which role gives me the better combination of interest, growth, autonomy, and identity fit over the next few years?”

If you want, I can help you build a **decision matrix** for the Google vs Anthropic choice and pressure-test it with you.

answer:
Short version: **the research does not really support the idea that “interesting work barely matters.”** If anything, it generally suggests the opposite:

- **Interesting / meaningful / intrinsically motivating work tends to be associated with higher job satisfaction and better well-being.**
- But **it is not the only thing that matters**, and once you already have a fairly good job, the **incremental happiness gain from “even more interesting” work may be smaller than people expect**.
- In practice, **autonomy, growth, manager quality, colleagues, workload, and whether the role fits your values/identity** often matter as much or more.

So if your question is:

> “Is there evidence that people are not much happier when they choose the most interesting job possible?”

I’d say: **not as a general claim**. The literature mostly says interesting/meaningful work helps. But there *is* evidence consistent with a more nuanced claim:

> **People often overestimate how much one salient feature of a job will determine their happiness**, because they adapt and because other features end up mattering a lot.

## What research broadly says

### 1. Intrinsically motivating work usually does improve satisfaction
A lot of organizational psychology points in this direction.

Two big frameworks:

- **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they involve things like skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback.
- **Self-Determination Theory**: people do better when work supports **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**.

“Interesting work” usually overlaps strongly with:
- autonomy
- challenge
- learning
- sense of progress
- use of strengths

Those are all pretty reliably linked to:
- job satisfaction
- engagement
- lower burnout risk
- sometimes overall life satisfaction too

### 2. Meaningful work matters, but it is not everything
There is also a large literature on **meaningful work**, and it generally finds positive links with:
- satisfaction
- engagement
- commitment
- psychological well-being

But meaningful or interesting work can coexist with:
- stress
- long hours
- politics
- pressure
- identity entanglement

So “interesting” is not a magic shield.

### 3. People adapt
A common finding in happiness research is **adaptation**: people get used to improved circumstances.

That doesn’t mean job content stops mattering. It means:
- the boost from a new exciting role may fade
- day-to-day lived conditions matter a lot
- recurring annoyances or benefits often dominate

So:
- A job that is 20% more interesting but 50% worse on stress, hours, manager quality, or culture may not make you happier.
- Conversely, a job that is somewhat less interesting but gives you a valued identity shift—like leading a team—could increase satisfaction.

### 4. People are bad at affective forecasting
Research in psychology also suggests people are often not great at predicting what will make them happy.

They tend to:
- overweight the most salient difference
- underweight adaptation
- underweight mundane repeated experiences

In your case, salient differences are:
- “Google research is more interesting”
- “Anthropic lets me lead a team”

But what may matter more after 6–12 months:
- How much time is spent in meetings vs technical work
- Whether you admire your manager
- Whether the team is easy or painful to work with
- Whether leadership there is actually empowering
- Whether “less interesting” work becomes interesting because you own a team and shape direction
- Whether leaving Google changes your long-term option value

## The most relevant nuance for you

You are not choosing between:
- boring warehouse work vs fascinating research

You are choosing between:
- **one already very good, intellectually rich job**
- **another high-status, high-agency job with leadership growth**

That matters a lot.

If someone already has a highly interesting job, then the question is less:
> “Does interesting work matter?”

and more:
> “How much more happiness do I get from marginally more interesting work versus leadership, compensation, scope, and future trajectory?”

Research is much less likely to give a clean universal answer there.

My guess from the literature would be:

- If **intellectual interest** is your core source of energy and identity, giving it up will probably cost you something real.
- If **leading people** is a genuine long-term aspiration, then finally getting that experience may be deeply satisfying in a way that standard “job interest” measures don’t capture well.
- Since both jobs are likely already above threshold on compensation/prestige, your long-run satisfaction may be driven more by:
  - autonomy
  - growth
  - people
  - stress
  - mission fit
  - how your weeks actually feel

## A useful way to frame this

Instead of asking:

> “Will I be happier in the most interesting job?”

ask:

> “What will my average Tuesday feel like in each role?”

That question often predicts happiness better.

For each job, estimate:

1. **How many hours per week will feel energizing?**
2. **How often will I feel dread/stress/friction?**
3. **Will I be learning what I want to learn?**
4. **Will I be becoming the person I want to become?**
5. **Would I regret not trying leadership now?**
6. **If this role were at equal pay and equal prestige, which would I choose?**

That last one is especially clarifying.

## A rough evidence-based takeaway

If I had to compress the research into one sentence:

> **Interesting work usually does contribute meaningfully to well-being, but maximizing interestingness is not the same as maximizing happiness.**

That’s probably the right answer.

Not because interesting work is overrated in general, but because:
- there are diminishing returns,
- people adapt,
- and other job attributes can dominate.

## My practical read on your situation

You wrote:

- Google: “very interesting stuff”
- Anthropic: “a bit higher salary”
- “leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do”
- but “much less interesting”

That makes this sound less like a compensation decision and more like a **development/identity decision**.

The real question may be:

> Which do I value more over the next 2–4 years:  
> deep individual-contributor research work, or building management/leadership capital?

If leadership is something you’ve “always wanted,” that is not a minor perk. It may represent:
- a new skill stack
- a new source of meaning
- a valuable career option
- information about yourself you can’t get any other way

That can be worth a lot, even if the technical content is less interesting.

On the other hand, if “much less interesting” means:
- you’ll be bored most days,
- the mission doesn’t resonate,
- you’ll miss hands-on technical work intensely,

then the novelty of leading may wear off and you may feel you traded away the part of work that actually energizes you.

## A decision heuristic I’d use

Ask yourself:

### Option A: Stay at Google
What is the main upside?
- intellectually compelling work
- maybe stronger daily flow
- maybe lower regret if research itself is your main love

What is the main risk?
- delaying leadership
- wondering “what if”
- staying in a comfort zone

### Option B: Go to Anthropic
What is the main upside?
- real leadership experience
- some extra pay
- maybe accelerated career growth
- learning whether management fits you

What is the main risk?
- lower day-to-day intellectual enjoyment
- becoming more managerial than you want
- discovering you value research craftsmanship more than leading

Then ask:

> Which regret feels worse in 3 years?

- “I didn’t take the chance to lead when it came.”
- “I gave up work I loved for status/trajectory.”

That’s probably more useful than trying to infer a general law from happiness research.

## My bottom line

- **No, I don’t think the evidence says interesting work barely matters.**
- **Yes, I do think the evidence suggests people often overrate a single job dimension and underrate fit, autonomy, relationships, and adaptation.**
- Since both of your options are already unusually good, **the marginal happiness difference will likely depend more on your personal values than on any generic finding.**

If you want, I can help you make this concrete with a **decision matrix** tailored to your case: interest, leadership growth, compensation, future optionality, stress, and regret.

answer:
Short version: the research does **not** really say “interesting work doesn’t matter.” It says something more nuanced:

1. **Interesting/challenging/meaningful work reliably improves job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Its effect on overall life happiness is real but smaller than people often expect.**
3. **After a job is “interesting enough,” other factors often matter as much or more**: autonomy, manager quality, coworkers, workload, stress, and fit with your values and identity.

So the evidence is closer to **“don’t maximize interestingness at all costs”** than to **“interestingness barely matters.”**

## What the research generally finds

### 1) Interesting work does matter
A lot of organizational psychology points this way.

- **Job Characteristics Model** (Hackman & Oldham): jobs with more **skill variety, autonomy, task significance, and feedback** tend to produce more motivation and satisfaction.
- A well-known **meta-analysis on work design** (Humphrey, Nahrgang, & Morgeson, 2007) found that things like **autonomy, task variety, and significance** are positively associated with job satisfaction, motivation, and lower strain.
- **Self-Determination Theory** (Deci & Ryan) also supports this: work is better for well-being when it supports **autonomy, competence, and relatedness**.

So if by “interesting” you mean intellectually engaging, meaningful, and varied, the literature mostly says: **yes, that usually helps.**

### 2) But it affects “job satisfaction” more than “overall happiness”
This is the important distinction.

Research on **job satisfaction vs life satisfaction** usually finds a **moderate**, not overwhelming, relationship. People who like their jobs are generally happier with life, but work is only one domain of life.

That means:

- A more interesting job can make your **workdays** better.
- But it may not transform your **whole-life happiness** as much as you imagine.

This is one reason people sometimes overstate the importance of maximizing interest alone.

### 3) People often overpredict the happiness impact of salient job features
This is very consistent with work on **affective forecasting** and the **focusing illusion**.

When making a decision, people tend to overweight the most salient aspect:
- “This work is fascinating”
- “This brand is prestigious”
- “This comp is higher”
- “I’ll finally be leading a team”

But day-to-day well-being often depends a lot on less glamorous things:
- What your Tuesdays look like
- How much autonomy you have
- Whether meetings drain you
- Whether your manager is good
- Whether the culture is humane
- Whether you end the day energized or fried

So yes: there *is* research support for the idea that people may **overestimate** how much choosing the “most interesting possible job” will improve happiness.

### 4) There are diminishing returns
Some models of work and well-being, like **Warr’s vitamin model**, suggest that some good job features help up to a point and then plateau, or even turn into stress in excess.

Translated:
- Going from dull to genuinely engaging: big improvement.
- Going from very interesting to maximally fascinating: often a much smaller gain.
- Meanwhile, losing control, sleep, social support, or work-life balance can hurt a lot.

So the “most interesting possible job” is not automatically the one that maximizes well-being.

### 5) Fit matters more than abstract interestingness
This is maybe the biggest practical point.

Research on **person-job fit** suggests satisfaction depends heavily on whether the role matches:
- your interests
- your values
- your strengths
- your desired identity
- your preferred way of working

For example:
- If you deeply want to **lead and mentor**, then a team lead role may actually be highly satisfying even if the technical content is less interesting.
- If what you really love is deep individual technical work, then a management-ish role can look great on paper and still make you less happy.

So the question is not just “Which job is more interesting?” but:
**“Which job is more aligned with the kind of work-self I want to become?”**

## What this implies for your decision

Your tradeoff is not really:

- **interesting work** vs **money**

It sounds more like:

- **more interesting technical content now**
vs
- **leadership growth, more money, and a role identity you’ve wanted**

Research would say both sides matter.

If I had to compress the evidence into one sentence:

> People are usually better off choosing a job that is sufficiently interesting **and** high in autonomy, fit, good relationships, and sustainable stress — rather than maximizing interestingness alone.

## A useful heuristic
Ask not:

- “Which job sounds more exciting?”

Ask:

- “In which job will my average week feel better?”
- “Which role gives me more autonomy and energy?”
- “Which kind of hard do I want: hard technical problems, or hard people/leadership problems?”
- “Which tradeoff will I be prouder of in 2 years?”
- “If compensation were equal, what would I choose?”
- “If titles were hidden, what daily life would I choose?”

That gets closer to what predicts actual well-being.

## My best answer to your research question
If your claim is:

> “People are not much happier when they choose the most interesting job possible.”

I’d say the literature supports a **weaker version**:

> People are somewhat happier when their work is interesting, but usually **not enough** to justify maximizing interestingness at the expense of other major job qualities.

So:
- **Interesting work matters** — probably more than cynics claim.
- **Maximizing interestingness is overrated** — probably more than idealists claim.
